coverage_notes: Strong coverage of novice runner injury epidemiology (3 papers), beginner program effectiveness and adherence (3 papers), physiological adaptations in novice runners (1 paper), and health outcomes from running in previously inactive adults (1 meta-analysis). Gap: no RCT specifically comparing run-walk interval protocols to continuous running in true beginners; the run-walk evidence is embedded within broader beginner program designs.

papers.title: Effectiveness of Start to Run, a 6-week training program for novice runners, on increasing health-enhancing physical activity: a controlled study
papers.notes: Evaluated a 6-week Start to Run beginner program in the Netherlands. Participants showed significant increases in vigorous-intensity physical activity and sports activity vs. controls, with 69% still running 4.5 months post-program. Demonstrates that structured beginner running programs produce lasting behavior change.

papers.title: Couch-to-5k or Couch to Ouch to Couch!? Who Takes Part in Beginner Runner Programmes in the UK and Is Non-Completion Linked to Musculoskeletal Injury?
papers.journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
papers.notes: First major study examining Couch-to-5K programme completion and injury. Only 27.3% completed the programme; 19% experienced musculoskeletal injuries. Previous injury was the strongest predictor of new injury (OR 7.56). Dropout themes included injury, negative emotions, and programme design issues. Highlights that C25K lacks an evidence base despite popularity.

papers.title: The Start-to-Run Distance and Running-Related Injury Among Obese Novice Runners: A Randomized Trial
papers.journal: International Journal of Sports Physical Therapy
papers.notes: RCT comparing 3 km/week vs 6 km/week start-to-run distance in obese novice runners (BMI 30-35). The lower-distance group had 31.2% fewer injuries and significantly fewer overuse symptoms (cumulative risk difference -47.8%). Critical evidence for conservative volume progression in heavier beginners.

papers.title: Incidence of Running-Related Injuries Per 1000 h of running in Different Types of Runners: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
papers.notes: Meta-analysis of 13 studies quantifying injury incidence per 1000 hours across runner types. Novice runners had the highest injury rate (17.8 per 1000h) compared to recreational runners (7.7 per 1000h). Establishes that beginners face more than double the injury risk of experienced recreational runners, which is essential context for coaching AI to manage training load conservatively.

papers.title: Predictors of Running-Related Injuries Among 930 Novice Runners: A 1-Year Prospective Follow-up Study
papers.notes: Large prospective cohort (930 novice runners, 155,318 km tracked) identifying injury predictors. BMI >30, age 45-65, non-competitive behavior, and previous non-running injuries increased injury risk. BMI <20 was protective. Provides key risk stratification data for personalizing beginner training plans.

papers.title: No effect of a graded training program on the number of running-related injuries in novice runners: a randomized controlled trial
papers.notes: RCT comparing a 13-week graded program (10% rule) vs standard 8-week program in 532 novice runners. No difference in injury rates (~20% in both groups). Challenges the popular 10% weekly increase rule, suggesting that total training volume and individual factors may matter more than progression rate alone for injury prevention in beginners.

papers.title: Meta-Analyses of the Effects of Habitual Running on Indices of Health in Physically Inactive Adults
papers.notes: Meta-analysis of 49 RCTs (22,380 records screened) on health effects of running in previously inactive adults. After ~1 year of training: -3.3 kg body mass, -2.7% body fat, -6.7 bpm resting HR, +7.1 ml/min/kg VO2max, improved lipid profiles. Longer training produced larger benefits. Provides the evidence base for communicating health benefits to beginner runners.

papers.title: Prospective study of beginner running groups: psychological predictors and outcomes of participation
papers.notes: Prospective study of 141 beginner running group members (63% low exercise at entry). 75% of completers achieved 30-minute continuous running. Self-efficacy, adherence, and younger age predicted success. Significant improvements in mental wellbeing, running identity, and social physique anxiety. Shows that psychological factors are critical for beginner program design and retention.
